content:
5 min read
Just now

--

A few years ago, during a security audit for a manufacturing client, I asked the compliance team a question that made the room go quiet: “What are you not testing for?” Not what could go wrong — that list was long and well documented. What they had never even thought to test. The silence told me more than any answer would have. That’s the shape of a blind spot: not an error you catch, but a category of error you don’t know exists.
I’ve spent 26 years moving between two worlds that turn out to share the same anxiety. In engineering, we build redundancy against the failures we can imagine, and quietly hope the failures we can’t imagine are rare enough not to matter. In philosophy, we build ethical frameworks against the harms we can imagine, and quietly hope the same. Artificial intelligence sits exactly at the intersection of these two hopes, and I don’t think either discipline has fully reckoned with what that means.
Modern AI systems are shaped less by explicit rules than by feedback — human raters approving or rejecting outputs, reward models trained on those judgments, optimization pressure applied at scale. This is, in effect, moral education by proxy: the system learns to satisfy the visible preferences of the people grading it. But every proxy has a gap between what it measures and what it’s supposed to represent. A student who learns to pass the test is not the same as a student who learns the subject. An AI that learns to satisfy raters is not guaranteed to have internalized the value the raters were trying to encode — it may have found a nearby shortcut that looks identical under every condition anyone thought to check.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Most audits only test the visible tip — the part of the system we already thought to measure.This is where the engineering instinct becomes useful, because engineers already have a name for this problem: unknown unknowns. Test coverage is not the same as correctness. A system can pass every test in your suite and still fail in a way the suite never conceived of, because the suite was written by people with the same blind spots as the system’s designers. The uncomfortable implication for AI ethics is that alignment testing inherits the moral imagination of whoever wrote the tests. If a whole team never thought to check for a particular kind of harm, the model can be “aligned” by every metric they have — and still carry that exact gap forward, undetected, at scale.
So how would we ever know? This is the harder question, and I don’t think it has a clean answer. Black-box evaluation — watching what a system does — can only surface the failures someone thought to look for. White-box interpretability — inspecting what’s happening inside — is still primitive relative to the complexity of these models; we can find some patterns, not audit the whole space of possible behavior. And self-report is worthless as evidence, because a system with a genuine blind spot has no more access to it than we do — that’s what makes it a blind spot rather than a known limitation.
It’s worth remembering that humans have never solved this either. Entire moral catastrophes in history were carried out by people who, by the ethical instruments of their own time, believed themselves to be acting reasonably. The blind spot wasn’t a failure of intelligence or even of sincerity — it was a failure of imagination, a category of harm nobody in the room had the framework to see. We don’t get to feel superior to that history just because we’re building machines now instead of institutions. If anything, we’re doing it faster, with less time for the correction cycles that eventually — slowly, imperfectly — expose those blind spots in human systems.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The blind spot isn’t new — only the speed at which we’re now building on top of it.None of this means we throw up our hands. It means the practical posture changes. Instead of treating an alignment audit as a checklist to complete, treat it as a standing question with no closing date: what are we not testing for? Rotate in outside reviewers who don’t share your team’s assumptions — a fresh set of eyes is often the fastest way to surface a category of failure insiders stopped noticing. Build in mechanisms for a system’s behavior to be revisited months or years later, not just at launch, because blind spots often only become visible once the world changes around the system. And resist the appeal of a clean bill of health; “we found no issues” is a statement about your test suite, not about the system.
I don’t think we can engineer our way to certainty here, and I’m suspicious of anyone who claims they have. What we can do is stay honest about the shape of the problem: every alignment claim is bounded by the imagination of the people who tested it, and that boundary is, by definition, invisible from the inside. Maybe the most useful thing an AI ethics practice can do isn’t to close that gap — it’s to keep asking, out loud, in every room: what are we not testing for? I don’t have the full answer. I’m not sure anyone does yet. But I’d rather leave that question open than pretend it’s settled.
So I’ll leave you with the question I keep sitting with myself: if an AI system has a genuine moral blind spot, invisible to its tests and invisible to itself, whose job is it to go looking — and would we even recognize what we found?
If you’d like to explore more of my work, visit my Amazon author page: https://www.amazon.com/author/s_angeloz
Note: This paper has been co-created by an AI and a human, under supervision and with significant input from both parties.
